**RwandAir Limited** is the national [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") [airline](/wiki/Airline "Airline") of [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), headquartered in [Kigali](/wiki/Kigali "Kigali") and operating from its primary hub at [Kigali International Airport](/wiki/Kigali_International_Airport "Kigali International Airport"). The airline operates flights 25 destinations across various regions, including [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"), [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa"), [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[6]](#cite_note-source1-6)

Within Africa, RwandAir connects major cities in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), and [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), among others. Internationally, it serves long-haul routes to destinations such as [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha"), and [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"). Through its expanding network, RwandAir continues to strengthen Rwanda's connectivity with regional and global markets.[[7]](#cite_note-source2-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Incorporation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Incorporation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Rwandair_Express_Boeing_737-500_Mutzair.jpg/250px-Rwandair_Express_Boeing_737-500_Mutzair.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rwandair_Express_Boeing_737-500_Mutzair.jpg)

A former Rwandair Express Boeing 737-500

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/RwandAir_Canadair_CL-600-2B19_Regional_Jet_CRJ-200LR_Mutzair.jpg/250px-RwandAir_Canadair_CL-600-2B19_Regional_Jet_CRJ-200LR_Mutzair.jpg)](/wiki/File:RwandAir_Canadair_CL-600-2B19_Regional_Jet_CRJ-200LR_Mutzair.jpg)

A former RwandAir Bombardier CRJ200LR

After the [1994 genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide"), the government took several attempts to revive the former national carrier [Air Rwanda](/wiki/Air_Rwanda "Air Rwanda") that ceased operations during the genocide. Various private companies showed interest in partnering with the government, and Uganda-based [SA Alliance Air](/wiki/Alliance_Air_(Uganda) "Alliance Air (Uganda)") ran the company from 1997 to 2000.[[8]](#cite_note-Six-8) After SA Alliance ceased operations, the government of Rwanda took over the Rwandan operations and re-branded the airline, to ensure its continuity. RwandAir began operations on 1 December 2002 as the new national carrier for Rwanda under the name **Rwandair Express** (with passenger air transportation as the core activity). In 2016, RwandAir received [International Air Transport Association's](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association "International Air Transport Association") Safety Audit for Ground Operations (ISAGO).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Re-branding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Re-branding")]

The airline began to expand regionally and by 2009 the network included [Dar-es-Salaam](/wiki/Dar-es-Salaam "Dar-es-Salaam"), [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"), and domestic destinations such as [Gisenyi](/wiki/Gisenyi "Gisenyi"). In March 2009, the airline registered the new trademark *RwandAir Limited*, which is its current operating name. In June 2009, the airline officially re-branded from Rwandair Express to RwandAir, because the new name implied a large, serious airline, while the "Express" in the former name implied a small regional operation.[[10]](#cite_note-Eight-10)

In May 2010, Rene Janata became the [CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO"), introducing a frequent flyer program and developing the airline to become a network carrier. In October 2010, John Mirenge became the new CEO of RwandAir.[[11]](#cite_note-Nine-11)

### 2010–2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2010–2015")]

In July 2010, the first of RwandAir's new Boeing 737-500s arrived. The second one arrived on 20 October 2010. Both were leased from [General Electric Capital Aviation Services](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") (GECAS). Each has a two-class configuration with 12 business class seats and 90 economy class seats.[[12]](#cite_note-Ten-12)

In August 2011, the airline took delivery of their first aircraft purchased directly from an airline manufacturer. All prior aircraft operated by RwandAir had been either leased or bought as a second-hand aircraft. The aircraft that was purchased is a Boeing 737-800 with Sky Interior, also known as the [Boeing 737 Next Generation](/wiki/Boeing_737_Next_Generation "Boeing 737 Next Generation"), and was the only one operating among [African](/wiki/Africa "Africa") airlines. The flight departed from Boeing Field in [Seattle, Washington](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), at 5:30 PM PST. It made its first stop in [Keflavík International Airport](/wiki/Keflav%C3%ADk_International_Airport "Keflavík International Airport") in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), then it headed for a second stop to [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). It finally arrived in [Kigali](/wiki/Kigali "Kigali"), Rwanda, after a 20-hour flight.[[13]](#cite_note-Eleven-13)

In October 2011, RwandAir took delivery of their second Boeing Next Generation 737-800. During January 2012, the airline disposed of the two [CRJ200](/wiki/CRJ200 "CRJ200") aircraft it owned, in anticipation of acquiring two CRJ-900NGs.[[14]](#cite_note-Twelve-14)

In February 2013, John Mirenge announced that the airline would fly to Accra, Cape Town, Harare, Juba, and Zanzibar.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In May 2015, RwandAir officially became an [IATA](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association "International Air Transport Association") member.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 2015 - present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2015 - present")]

In 2017, the [Government of Benin](/wiki/Government_of_Benin "Government of Benin") granted RwandAir [seventh freedom](/wiki/Freedoms_of_the_air#Seventh_freedom "Freedoms of the air") rights to operate direct flights from Benin. RwandAir plans to base two Boeing 737 aircraft at [Cotonou International Airport](/wiki/Cadjehoun_Airport "Cadjehoun Airport") in Benin.[[17]](#cite_note-Fifteen-17)

In February 2020, two months after [Qatar Airways](/wiki/Qatar_Airways "Qatar Airways") purchased a 60% stake in Rwanda's [Bugesera International Airport](/wiki/Bugesera_International_Airport "Bugesera International Airport"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) the Qatari state-owned airline purchased a 49% stake in RwandAir.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

**Flight Pass**[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 2019, RwandAir entered into a partnership with USA-based Optiontown to launch a prepaid flight subscription platform called Flight Pass, which enables customers to pre-purchase RwandAir flights at the best available price and decide when they want to travel at a later date.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In September 2022, the airline's intentions in joining [Oneworld](/wiki/Oneworld "Oneworld"), with a sponsorship from [Qatar Airways](/wiki/Qatar_Airways "Qatar Airways"), were announced.[[23]](#cite_note-23) This would make RwandAir the third airline to enter an airline alliance in East Africa, after [Ethiopian Airlines](/wiki/Ethiopian_Airlines "Ethiopian Airlines") ([Star Alliance](/wiki/Star_Alliance "Star Alliance")) and [Kenya Airways](/wiki/Kenya_Airways "Kenya Airways") ([SkyTeam](/wiki/SkyTeam "SkyTeam")), and second African airline after [Royal Air Maroc](/wiki/Royal_Air_Maroc "Royal Air Maroc") to join Oneworld.

## Corporate affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Corporate affairs")]

### Ownership and management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ownership and management")]

Rwandair is owned 100 percent by the Government of Rwanda.[[24]](#cite_note-DOTOST2017-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Seventeen-25) As of May 2021, an agreement to sell a 49 percent stake to [Qatar Airways](/wiki/Qatar_Airways "Qatar Airways") is said to be in the final stages.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The government hoped to privatise the airline after 2013, once it became profitable. However, the process had been abandoned in 2008 after it emerged that nobody at the time was willing to offer the amount expected from the sale.[[28]](#cite_note-Eighteen-28)

RwandAir's board of directors is responsible for ensuring that the airline follows a suitable corporate governance framework to ensure the creation and protection of value for the shareholder. [Patricie Uwase](/wiki/Patricie_Uwase "Patricie Uwase") is currently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the chairman of RwandAir since September 2021; the long-time aviation veteran [Girma Wake](/wiki/Girma_Wake "Girma Wake") was chairman from 2012 to 2017.[[4]](#cite_note-Two-4) Yvonne Manzi Makolo is the current[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] CEO, having been promoted from deputy CEO in charge of Corporate Affairs in April 2018. She replaced acting CEO Col. Chance Ndagano.[[5]](#cite_note-Twenty-5)

### Business trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Business trends")]

### Head office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RwandAir&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Head office")]

The airline has its head office on the top floor of the main building of [Kigali International Airport](/wiki/Kigali_International_Airport "Kigali International Airport") in [Kigali](/wiki/Kigali "Kigali"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Newheadoffice-50) The airline previously had its head office in Centenary House in Kigali.[[51]](#cite_note-FI-51) The airline began moving its operations from Centenary House to the airport on Friday 14 May 2010. The airline was scheduled to be moved in by Monday 17 May 2010.[[50]](#cite_note-Newheadoffice-50) At one previous point the airline had its head office in the Telcom House.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
